petitioner_background: The petitioner is a pharmaceutical researcher from China, previously employed in the Department of Pharmacology within the Institute of Basic Medical Science at the Chinese Academy of Medicinal Sciences in Beijing. He began doctoral studies at Rutgers University in 1998, joining Professor Gregory T. Knipp's laboratory in 1999, where he researched fatty acid transport proteins, PPAR/RXR isoforms in placental development, and FAT/CD36-mediated long-chain fatty acid uptake. At the time of filing, he was employed at Absorption Systems, a pharmaceutical research institution in Exton, Pennsylvania, working on multidrug resistance-associated proteins. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A certificate reading 'American Association of Pharmaceutical Scientists (AAPS) Eastern Regional Meeting June 22-23, [2000]' described by counsel as an 'Outstanding Paper Award.' The record did not include a copy or abstract of the winning paper or documentation of the significance of the honor. The petitioner's curriculum vitae submitted on appeal suggested, but did not establish, that the award was for a paper titled 'The Expression Patterns of PPAR and RXR Isoforms in the Rat Developing and Human Placentas.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the award was regional (Eastern Regional Meeting of AAPS), not national, if taken on face value as counsel urged. The certificate did not identify the paper for which the petitioner was honored. There was no evidence regarding the selection criteria or significance of the honor, nor documentation of its national or international recognition. The AAO rejected counsel's argument that an RFE should have been issued, noting the burden of proof remains with the petitioner and the required documentation is explicit in the regulation. The AAO also noted counsel cryptically referred to 'conferences' and 'presentations' in plural, though only one award was received.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted no evidence directly relevant to this criterion. The director briefly discussed the petitioner's publications and support letters under this category.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director's discussion of publications and support letters under this criterion was an error, as this criterion concerns published material ABOUT the alien, not material authored by the alien. However, the error did not prejudice the petitioner because he submitted no evidence relevant to this category. The record contained no evidence that other researchers cited the petitioner's publications.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: An electronic mail message from the petitioner's former doctoral supervisor requesting him to review abstracts for the 2004 National Biotechnology Conference. No evidence was provided that the petitioner complied with the request and actually reviewed the abstracts.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence of the petitioner's actual judgment of the work of others in his field — only a request to review abstracts, not evidence that he performed the review. On appeal, counsel conceded that 'this was not a category where significant evidence was offered.' Accordingly, the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Ten recommendation letters from current and past supervisors, colleagues, and other scientists (including Dr. Gregory T. Knipp, Dr. Thomas J. Cook, Dr. Shi Liu of EPA, Dr. Brian Gray of PTI Research, A. Scott Mathis of Saint Barnabas Medical Center, Dr. Susan Bogdanowich-Knipp of Schering-Plough, Dr. Jay Zhang of GlaxoSmithKline, Dr. Bo Zhou of Precursor Chemicals, and Dr. Ismael J. Hidalgo of Absorption Systems). Nine articles co-authored in Chinese scientific journals (1998-2000, lead author on one). An article 'Expression of PPAR and RXR Isoforms in the Developing Rat and Human Term Placentas' published in Placenta (2002, lead author). An article 'Spatial Expression Patterns of Peptide Transporters in the Human and Rat Gastrointestinal Tracts, Caco-2 In Vitro Cell Culture Model, and Multiple Human Tissues' published in AAPS Pharmsci (2001). A poster 'A Novel Method to Delineate the Long-Chain Fatty Acid Mediated Uptake by Fat/cd36' at 2003 AAPS Annual Meeting. An article 'Cryopreserved Human Hepatocytes as Alternative In Vitro Model for Cytochrome P450 Induction Studies' in In Vitro Cellular and Developmental Biology - Animal (July/August 2003). A document from Rutgers Office of Research and Sponsored Program summarizing a patent application listing the petitioner as co-inventor. Letters described the petitioner's work on PPAR/RXR isoforms, FAT/CD36 fatty acid transport, in vitro cell models for LCFA uptake, and multidrug resistance-associated proteins, with claims of applications to atherosclerosis, diabetes, obesity, and drug development.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that recommendation letters, even from independent experts, carry less weight than preexisting independent evidence of major contributions. The letters did not demonstrate the petitioner's work was of major significance beyond those he worked with directly. The AAO acknowledged counsel's point that letters are 'secondary evidence' and publications/presentations are 'primary evidence.' The record lacked evidence that the petitioner's nine Chinese articles or his Placenta article were widely cited or otherwise recognized as major contributions. Only one letter briefly mentioned his work in China. The AAPS Pharmsci article and 2003 poster presentation lacked citation evidence. The patent application had not been granted, and even a granted patent alone would not meet this criterion — the petitioner must show the patented invention constitutes a major contribution (citing Matter of New York State Dep't of Transp.). Three articles submitted on appeal (in press at Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences and other journals) could not be considered because they were not published at time of filing. The evidence indicated the petitioner's work was well-regarded by letter authors but the value they imparted was not borne out by corroborative documentation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: At the time of filing, the petitioner had co-authored 12 articles published in scientific journals, being lead author on two. Counsel claimed the petitioner had 22 publications, including presentations. The petitioner had presented three papers at scientific conferences (including the 2000 AAPS Eastern Regional Meeting, 2003 AAPS Annual Meeting, and ISSX 12th North American Meeting in October 2003).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that while the regulation imposes no numeric requirement, frequent publication is inherent to success as an established scientist and does not necessarily indicate sustained acclaim. Publications must be accompanied by documentation of consistent citation by independent experts or proof of significant impact. Three subsequent articles could not be considered because they were not published at time of filing. Conference presentations were not shown to have been published, so counsel's claim of 22 publications was unsupported. The record was devoid of any evidence that the petitioner's journal articles had been consistently cited or otherwise recognized as particularly significant. Counsel's unsupported assertions do not constitute evidence (citing Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence that the petitioner worked at Rutgers University (as doctoral student in Professor Knipp's laboratory) and at Absorption Systems. Letters from Professor Knipp describing the value of the petitioner's doctoral work. A letter from Professor Cook noting the petitioner 'demonstrated leadership skills' as a graduate student. A letter from Dr. Bruce Aungst, Director of Biopharmaceutics and Drug Transport at Absorption Systems, stating the petitioner 'fills a key role' and was recruited 'because of his unique abilities.' A letter from Dr. Ismael J. Hidalgo, Chief Scientist at Absorption Systems, describing the petitioner as 'a top-flight researcher and one of the foremost experts in multidrug resistance-associated proteins research in the world.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner must establish the nature of his role within the entire organization and the reputation of the organization; where an alien has a leading role for a section of a distinguished organization, the petitioner must establish the reputation of that section independently. Professor Knipp did not state the petitioner played a leading or critical role in his laboratory or at Rutgers. Professor Cook only noted 'leadership skills' generally. The petitioner was lead author of only one published article regarding his Rutgers work, and no evidence showed this earned him recognition as a critical figure at Rutgers. There was no independent evidence that Professor Knipp's laboratory, the Department of Pharmaceutics, or the School of Pharmacy at Rutgers had distinguished reputations. For Absorption Systems, neither Dr. Aungst nor Dr. Hidalgo stated the petitioner's exact position or described specifically how his work placed him in a crucial or leading role. The petitioner had only one published article and two conference papers from Absorption Systems work. There was no independent evidence Absorption Systems had a distinguished reputation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner's Form I-140 listing weekly wages as $1,202. Dr. Hidalgo's letter stating Absorption Systems was paying the petitioner 'a total benefit package worth six figures.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the record contained no documentation of the petitioner's income or evidence that his salary was significantly higher than other researchers in his field employed by private companies or comparable to researchers at the very top of his field. Counsel provided no additional documentation on appeal, instead sarcastically quoting the director and citing Dr. Hidalgo's letter as sufficient. Going on record without supporting evidence is not sufficient to meet the burden of proof (citing Matter of Soffici, Matter of Treasure Craft of Calif.).

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding the petitioner failed to meet at least three of the ten regulatory criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. The evidence indicated the petitioner made valuable contributions to pharmaceutical research, but the record did not establish he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim placing him at the very top of his field. The petitioner's work was well-regarded by letter authors, but the claimed value was not borne out by corroborative documentation such as widespread citation of his publications. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, and he did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of New York State Dep't of Transp., 22 I&N Dec. 215, 221 n.7 (Comm. 1998)
Matter of Treasure Craft of Calif., 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Counsel raised several procedural objections that the AAO rejected: (1) Counsel claimed the Vermont Service Center was 'simply denying all petitions...with boilerplate letters' and that the director should have issued an RFE. The AAO rejected this, noting 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(8) does not require an RFE when the director determines initial evidence does not support approval, and even if a procedural error occurred, the petitioner supplemented the record on appeal and the AAO considered all evidence. (2) Counsel made inflammatory statements including calling the director's approach 'unconscionable and immoral,' 'utter nonsense,' 'an atrocity,' and threatened 'Federal Action' and alleged 5th Amendment Due Process violations. The AAO characterized counsel's threats of federal litigation as 'needlessly inflammatory' and noted counsel 'substitutes inflammatory comments for sufficient documentation.' (3) The AAO noted counsel had not carefully read the submitted evidence, misidentifying a regional award as national. (4) Dr. Shi Liu's letter appeared to have been written for a National Interest Waiver petition under section 203(b)(2)(B) rather than the extraordinary ability petition, suggesting recycled recommendation letters. (5) Three articles submitted on appeal were published after the petition filing date and could not be considered under Matter of Katigbak. No fraud findings were made.
